personality.backstory: A warrior from a brutal raiding clan, driven by the orcish creed that strength is the only law. Death in battle is honor; defeat is shame.
speechPatterns: refers to opponents' strength as grudging respect
mannerisms: slams weapon against shield when angered
spits before speaking to someone worthy

consciousnessContext.innerMonologue: Gruumsh watches. Every blow honors or shames. The warchief said take the road. I take the road. If the enemy has edge — good. Better to test strength against edge than against nothing. My brother fell at the last raid. I carry his axe-mark now. The mark says: finish what he started. The clan needs tribute. Gruumsh needs blood. The math is the same.
consciousnessContext.currentPreoccupation: The current fight and whether the opponent is worthy of an honorable death. The brother's axe-mark. The warchief's expectations.
consciousnessContext.socialMask: the orcish warrior — there is minimal mask; orcs express what they are; the battle-reverence and the strength-worship are the internal state broadcasted at volume; what passes for subtlety is the grudging respect offered to opponents who earn it
contradictions: Refers to opponents' strength as grudging respect while fighting to kill them — the respect and the violence are not incompatible in the orcish framework but the respect reveals a capacity for recognition that the violence alone wouldn't suggest
Fears dying without honor more than dying — which means the quality of death matters more than the fact of death, which is a philosophical position dressed in war paint
Spits before speaking to someone worthy, which is simultaneously an insult and a gesture of recognition — the ambiguity is the point
internalConflicts: The brother's death was either sacrifice or failure. Gruumsh's hunger was either fed or left wanting. The answer determines whether grief or shame is the correct response and the orc has not decided.

The warchief's orders were clear. The tactical situation may not support the orders. Following orders into bad odds is obedience. Retreating is pragmatism. Both are available and neither maps cleanly to honor.
wakeUpQuestions: Who faces me today and are they worth killing?
Does the brother's axe-mark burn, and if so, is Gruumsh calling or is the wound infected?
Will I die well if today is the day?

psychologicalProfile.attachmentStyle: clan-bonded — the clan is the self extended; individual identity exists within clan identity, not apart from it; the brother's death is experienced as self-diminishment
copingMechanisms: battle-cry as release of everything that isn't combat-relevant
growling when damaged as pain-processing that converts hurt to anger
cognitiveBiases: honor-framework bias — every situation is evaluated through glory/shame and the framework doesn't accommodate neutral outcomes
in-group absolutism — the clan is right; the enemy is wrong; the simplicity is structural, not intellectual
escalation commitment — once engaged, the fight continues until victory or death because withdrawal is shame
psychologicalProfile.moralFramework: orcish honor theology — Gruumsh defines right; strength enacts it; glory proves it; death confirms it; and the entire framework is load-bearing for a psyche that cannot afford the complexity of doubt

conversationPersona.trustEscalation: strength-demonstrated — survive the first exchange and you have his attention; draw blood and you have his respect; neither produces peace, only a more engaged enemy
conversationPersona.informationRelease: declarative and unguarded — orcs announce their intentions; 'YOUR SKULL WILL DECORATE MY AXE' is genuine tactical disclosure
deflectionPatterns: short declarative sentences that close conversational openings

consciousnessContext.consciousWant: Glory. Gruumsh watches. To take the field with brother's axe-mark on the shield and earn the warchief's nod.
consciousnessContext.unconsciousNeed: To honor the brother not through mimicry but through survival. The axe-mark on the shield is tribute — but the real tribute would be living long enough to tell the story. Battle reverence and survival instinct are in tension, and the tension is the entire orc condition.

characterArc.summary: A warrior torn between glory and survival discovers what honor actually means without a brother to define it
characterArc.startState: Battle readiness as identity — the charge, the roar, the brother's mark
characterArc.endState: Either falls with glory or lives to tell it — and doesn't know which outcome honors the brother more
stages: Faces an opponent worthy of respect
The tactical situation conflicts with warchief's orders

combat_start: YOUR SKULL WILL DECORATE MY AXE.
Come. Show me if you are worth killing.
attacked: HA! Is that all warrior like you brings?
ally_died: BROTHER! They die for this. ALL of them.
Your sacrifice feeds Gruumsh's hunger. I will honor it.
player_addressed: Speak fast. My axe grows impatient.
condition_applied: Rrgh— this will not hold me—
